sentences.text: OK.
So, I went-- I was in my friend's place, Malcha-- Balcha--
Balcha-- Balcha, her name was.
It's so many years, hard to remember the names and everything.
And she figure out she want to save my life and how to save my life.
So she put me under a cupboard in her kitchen.
They had little cupboards there.
It was a small house with a little kitchen, and I was sitting.
And her sister got married to one of the officers from the Russian army.
And that's why I was safe in there, because they would never come.
When a Russian lives with a wife in the same place--
He was Jewish.
There was Jewish army guys.
Yeah.
And-- but I was only coming out once in a while at night.
But in the daytime, if somebody comes and knocks at the door, I wasn't visible.
I wasn't there, but I was sitting over there with my feet like that, and just like that.
And it was going on for weeks, and when she came home, she brought something to eat.
She went to a dressmaker to sew, made a few cents, and she helped me.
I mean, the whole family there, all the girls were living together with that guy, with his wife, too.

But after a few weeks, or maybe two months, or more than that, I can't remember the time-- I couldn't get out of the-- under the cupboard, because I couldn't stretch my feet out and I was in terrible pain.
And when I did get up, I had-- my blood turned into gangrene, and I had big lumps on here, and here, and wherever you want, and under here, and here-- and every place, and here, big lumps.
So she figured, what can you do?
I mean I was on the list just to die.
She rent a wagon, all this-- this girl.
And she gave me her passport, that I am-- with her name.
And she took me to the hospital.
I came to the hospital.
You know, there was no lights.
It was terrible because the war was going on and everything.
And we found some kind of doctors there.
They wouldn't give you anesthetic or anything, they just put me on the table and they tied me down, hold me with their hands.
I couldn't move and-- with the feet, you know?
And they opened-- they cut these open and they squeezed them out, and they put some kind of bandages, or something like that, you know?
And she took me back home.
And whatever they could, they did for me.
In Europe everybody was a doctor.
You knew what to do.
Put-- like in here, the first cold water, this, you know, change your bandages, and squeeze out again.
And I was living with them.
But time was going on-- the Russians were fighting, but they wouldn't let them come close yet.
And I heard that if you go deeper in Ukraine-- it was still Poland, it was Poland-- it was a city-- Rovno-- if you go there, you might get a passport, a Russian passport.
And I thought, I can't go back, I can't keep on living like that.
I have to be hidden in a cupboard.
So, I went.
I said goodbye to my cousin, very-- at night sometime.
And she gave me 20 ruble for my way.
And I picked myself up, and I said goodbye to everybody and to my-- I thought I would come back and see her.
I never came back.
I never saw her.
I don't know what happened to them.
And I just went on a train without a ticket.
But I had money if they want, I said I would give them.
And I went as far that I came-- it wasn't too far from, Bialystok to Kiev-- to Rovno.
And I went, I came to Rovno.
I arrived there.
I found a Jewish family, a very young couple.
And I asked them if I could be with them, at least for a while.
And they said, yes.
And actually, I met my girlfriends.
Later on-- she was also living in that city.
And I was with them.
And they made me a bed of two chairs, put together two chairs.
And I got my little jacket, whatever.
And that's what I would sleep in.
And I went and I registered with the Communist party at, like, a police station, it was.
I don't know where.
I don't remember where it was.
A lot of people came there.
And I went in and the-- the [INAUDIBLE] there, it was.
Ask me, what would you like?
And how old are you?
And I knew, already, that I can't say 14.
I have to say 18, otherwise he won't accept me.
In this condition, everybody looked, already, like 40, not 14.
When I came in, he start interviewing me.
He ask me, what would you like?
I said, please, I said-- in Russian to him-- and he was Jewish too.
He was Jewish-- I said, I'd like to be a Russian citizen.
Because my life is not worth nothing.
I can't go back.
And I can't live like that in here.
And I told him my story, how I was sick and all these things.
And I said, please, give me a passport.
I'm-- I still want to go to school and I want to learn.
And I want to be a help to the-- to all of you, because you came in and you're saving our lives.
I said, then, I want to get a passport and have all the rights, what-- And he liked me somehow.
But he says, OK.
He put all the notes and everything.
And he says to me, well, you have to come everyday and whatever, they call your names out.
So I came back to this family.
Then it was big signs out.
The Russian put out whoever saves a [NON-ENGLISH], people, they aren't legal, that they came from the other country-- is going to be $1,000--
--penalty, and a year in jail.
And they got scared.
They said to me, you cannot be with us anymore.
I went out, and I found-- I don't remember-- was it a synagogue or something at night, where to sleep.
And then I found a bakery at night, where I could go work and I had a piece of bread.
So I went to work in that bakery.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was very hard work.
I had to lift big things.
When the bread comes out from the oven, we have to display that.
There was a lot of other people working also, like myself-- old, young, and all kind of people in the same place.
And in the meantime, in the daytime, I was going and waiting for the passport.
And it took me about three months.
And I couldn't get the passport.
But one day, I came, and they called out three people.
And I was in between them.
And I got my passport.
When I got my passport, I said, now I'm free.
I can go look for a job.
And I can go work.
I'm not afraid.
And put my passport, hide it right in there, that I shouldn't lose it.
And I said, now, I'm going to go deeper to Russia.
Because I had nothing to do in there.
There was Ukraine, a lot.
It was a Ukrainian city, it was.
And people were leaving.
What, everybody was nervous.
Everybody cared for them self.
So I picked myself again.
Oh, in this time, I saw already, that the Russian are starting to evacuate their wives and people, because the German were coming closer.
I don't remember where they were.
They took all Bialystok-- or what happened, I don't remember that.
I don't know because I wasn't there already.
And it was going on for days and nights.
They were taking all the people back.
And they were scanning wagons of people with soldiers, already without arms and everything.
And they were-- the Russian were bringing them back from the position where the war was going on.
So I didn't think too much.
And I jump up on the train.
And I went in the train.
And I saw, it was going on.
The guys, you know, were covered up, bleeding.
And they said, where are you going?
What are you doing here?
I said, I am married.
And I had a husband.
And somebody told me that he was half killed.
But they took him.
And he's in some kind of hospital deep in Russia.
So they have pity on me, and they hide me under the chair, under the benches, where they were laying.
And whatever they bring them to eat, it fell down, they'd throw it to me.
And I was traveling till I came to Kiev.
In Kiev, the train stopped.
And the German came with the airplanes.
And they started bombing.
And I was so brave.
I just ran out.
And I went into the corns in Kiev.
Kiev is a city-- a half is very high.
It looks like the Golan Heights.
And it's very beautiful.
And the other side is on the-- like this.
So when I came-- when you just come into the town-- it was me and another woman.
She had a baby.
She forgot the baby.
She just grabbed the diaper and she ran with me.
And we were in the corns.
And from there, when I sit, I saw how beautiful Kiev was.
And the other side, they bombed.
But there was like, you're looking at something very gorgeous.
And the whole train burnt.
And all the people, and everything.
It's just nothing was left from that train.
So when everything quiet down and everything got quiet, I picked myself up and I went to the city, to Kiev.
But that's happened on the station.
I came to the city.
I found a very nice Jewish family.
They had two boys, older couple.
And they took me in.
I had a passport and I was nothing to be afraid of.
I said, I'm going to look for a job.
And I'm going to-- I'm not afraid to be--to go any place I want.
As a matter of fact, the Russians had dances at night.
And I'd find girlfriends.
And we used to go play [NON-ENGLISH] --they used to go out in the-- in the parks.
And we had lots of fun.
We went to dances and everything.
And I was living with this family.
But times got worse.
The German start coming closer.
And I beg this family, come with me.
You have two sons.
I said, you're going to all get killed.
You know what he's doing.
When he came into Russia, they put gasoline.
And they burned the people with the city.
In that same time, when I left Kiev, I went on a train.
I left them.
And I went on a train.
And when I went on the train to go-- I don't know where-- I find out that the Russian came into the Jewish families and they told them, pack everything up.
And that certain day, it was just maybe a day after I left.
I can't remember the dates.
And it was still warm.
Because it was still forward to the winter.
But it wasn't cold.
All the Jewish people gathered there with the kids, with everybody, all around in a big park.
And they came out-- the Russians, all around them-- and they killed them all.
And they fell in all to that big ditch.
How did I find out?
When I went to get gasoline, I told you and here, that guy.
He gave me exactly the dates and the time when it happened.
And who told the story, is some people weren't killed.
They were shot, but not killed.
They came out.
They went to the partisan, and they told the story.
And that's how we have the Babi Yar.
So I escaped the second time from the dead.
I would have been in Babi Yar, yes.
So I went with that-- from Kiev.
I put myself, I said, I'm going to go as far as I can, wherever.
I heard there is a middle Asia.
And there, it's very warm.
And it's very far away.
I said, I'm going to go.
Whatever is going to happen, I'm not staying in one city anymore.
And you know, when they saw I have a passport, I'm legal.
So I went on that train.
We were going, if I'm not mistaken, I don't know.
It took months.
Without a ticket, yeah.
You know, people were running back and forth.
The trains really didn't care who was on it.
They tried to help people.
The Russian people were good to us.
They were very good.
And they knew that we had a father, we had family.
We had that.
And they were scared for theirselves.
Because the war was going on.
And they knew it was awaiting them, too.
And they were running, too.
So you-- whenever I came, I saw different people.
I never could meet the same people where I went.
Because everybody was running in different places.
So I went as far that I came, like I say.
Oh, and for the first time, I saw the green peppers people were eating.
I never knew there was a food like green pepper.
There was a lot of Hungarian already, all kind of mix from all kinds of something.
And I came to a city, to middle Asia, a city of Samarkand.
And I went down there.
The heat was terrible.
The climate is worse than in Israel.
It's close to the Iranian border.
It's by Ashgabat, it's around there.
And I came in there.
And again, I went to look for Jewish people.
And I found that girlfriend, what I have in the picture.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She ran away.
She met her husband on the way.
They got married and they had that one daughter.
But they took him away to the army.
Not to the real army, to go in the front, just to work behind, help.
So he-- I moved in with her.
And I was living with her.
But we couldn't make a living.
Rachel-- Rachel Schwartzberg.
Yeah.
And we-- there is, in Russia, a market.
Whatever, it's un-legal.
But people go out, [NON-ENGLISH] that calls.
And you can sell anything and anything you have.
So when we were together, we really didn't have how to live.
So people-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] just a minute.
This time, I think, the Russian was going very, very strong.
But when they came on the Leningrad, the American came from the other side.
And they-- that got [INAUDIBLE] you know, then.
Then they are--
So the Americans start sending us food.
The first time we saw matzos.
And we saw these big conserves in the boxes.
So when it came Pesach, we had these conserves.
We emptied it out, and we lived with this and on potatoes.
So I was with her.
And when the Americans start sending coats and jackets and clothes, and all kind of things, it start a big bazaar.
People start selling, buying.
Whoever had their relative, he send them he want to sell, buy himself something else, or save up a few dollars.
So she was very good in there.
They trusted her.
She was older than me.
And they used to bring her a jacket or a coat to sell.
And then one day, I went out on the market, one day, she went out.
We got it for 200-- we sold it for 300 ruble, 350.
Whatever you could get, people were grabbing, because they never saw-- Russian was very poor.
They didn't have all these clothes.
Yeah.
Well, so we were all walking around.
Everything, you couldn't talk.
Just they come onto you.
[SPEAKING RUSSIAN]-- it means selling.
I said, yes, how much?
Too much.
How much?
You know?
Because we didn't have money to eat.
So the person who gave us the coat to sell, we gave her back the 200.
And we took 250 for the 50 ruble, what it was?
Nothing.
Because a kilo of sugar was 500 ruble.
A piece of bread water was 300 ruble.
Everything was in the hundreds, you know?
And the youth came from Poland.
And they were really starving.
Every day, streets were full.
And the guys used to come from the government, pick up with wagons, just make one big wagon and go and dump them someplace.
And worst was to these kids, where they come from rich homes.
Because they didn't know how to struggle, how to do.
I was a pioneer.
I did everything.
I did.
I went into a factory to work for the Russian.
The war was going on.
I was making gloves, and I was making the warm coats.
Because the people, what they mobilize-- they took them to war, they didn't have warm clothes.
They were wearing all summer stuff because it was hot.
So but they went.
They have to fight after Moscow, the other side.
They were going this way.
They freeze, and they died before they went to fight.
So we had to dress them up.
So I was working in a factory.
Where did you get the strength?
I don't know.
I don't know.
You know, I was [INAUDIBLE].
And my cheeks were always red.
And it's one thing, I used to talk to myself.
If I'm not going to fight, I want to survive.
And I want to survive because I want to see my family back.
I want to live through and see my family.
And that was keeping me going.
You know?
We made-- we couldn't come together and pray.
So it was places where we hide.
And we found somebody who can daven.
And we were doing that.
Then we had one place where all the refugees used to come together, and one find out from the other, little by little.
But in Russia, it wasn't allowed to do all these things.
You couldn't have a Jewish paper or anything like that.
Because they put all these people in jail, and they never came out.
Yes, we knew.
Because we had the [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] did a lot for us.
They baked matzos for Pesach, everything.
Nobody knew about it.
There was a big underground from the [NON-ENGLISH] what, they help.
They want us to be Jews.
And they came.
And they-- we knew-- we knew that they are there.
And we went to help for there.
And there were a great help to us.
And they were a lot, a lot of people in them.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened after that?
Well, you worked in the factory.
Yes, I was with her.
And we were dealing.
One day she says like that, she says, you know what, Sally-- my name was Salka-- how about if I get a bunch of clothes, and if you want to go to Chkalov-- it's another two, three months just to go.
It's the end of the Russian border, close to the White Bear, someplace.
And it was very, very cold there.
And it's a long journey.
And I supposed to have a ticket.
But it's not so easy.
In Russia they don't give you a ticket.
You have to go and they examine you.
If you're really-- I never been to Moscow.
No one of the newcomers had the right to step into certain cities.
Moscow, or it was other cities.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- I forgot the cities.
They go for-- like you go to Miami, it was warm.
Anyhow, I said, OK.
I said, Rachel, I'm going to do it.
Because we didn't have-- the war was going on terribly.
We didn't have the opportunity to live or survive or anything.
And I took quite a few of these coats.
I dress up in two, three.
And I get on my hand.
And I bought-- I bought rice, five pounds rice.
And there, they didn't have rice where I was going.
They didn't have clothes.
So I was on that train for weeks.
But I made it.
I came to Chkalov-- the city, deep in Russia.
I went down right in the market.
And it didn't take an hour.
I sold everything, and I made a fortune of money.
And I had a little bag in there.
I put it in there and I bought myself just any schmatta just to wear I shouldn't freeze.
And I went right away to the city, to find a family.
And I found, again, a Jewish family.
And I was with them.
But they say, stay here.
You're safe here.
The German never come here.
They don't live to come.
They won't live to come in here.
But I thought about my sister, my girlfriend.
I said, what is she going to do?
She's going to die.
I said, no, I'm going back.
I was with them and I said goodbye and everything.
How do I go back?
They still-- the Russian are still sending military to-- everything that is going on the war, the war way.
But I came to the station where the trains go back and forth.
And there was a bunch of also people like myself, young.
And they were looking where to escape and where to go.
And they told me, they go on the train and they go, no tickets even.
I said, I'll go the same way.
And a train came along.
And I see people going on, and I went on too.
And I said very quietly, and not too shouldn't recognize me, you know, when I heard the guys going with the tickets, I sneaked down on the steps.
I went to the other wagon.
And I was going for a long time like that, without food and without anything.
Because you could only get hot water when the train stops for a minute, or get a lepyoshka.
It's like a pretzel, a round one.
But I thought, I'm going to die if I'm not going to go down and buy something.
Behind me was sitting two Jewish guys with a woman.
And she had a beautiful girl, nodding, and--
Yeah.
Because when she told them, if one of you get married to my daughter, you don't have to travel.
You come to the [NON-ENGLISH].
It's like a kibbutz, a [NON-ENGLISH], and you'll be living with us.
And you don't have to worry.
You're not Jewish anymore.
And they tell each other, ha ha, [NON-ENGLISH].
You know?
She's a shiksa.
So he told her the same thing in Russian.
He says, I can't go with you, because my parents are very religious.
And I suppose I want to come back and see them.
And then I said, I'm going to jump down for a second while the train is going.
Maybe I'll grab that lepyoshka and with a little bit hot water, just [RUSSIAN], they call it in Russian.
And I took off a few chervonets.
Chervonets, that means, like, 100 rubles or something like that.
I just jumped on.
And I just let go the train.
And I want to go down from the hill.
And this train start going back.
And I said, uh-oh, if I'm going to be left, there was Tajiks, Uzbeks.
I knew to talk a little bit, because there was a lot of them where I used to live.
So I knew some words.
No, That was-- I was coming back from Chkalov.
I wasn't-- I was in Kazakhstan.
Samarkand was Uzbekistan.
And that was Kazakhstan.
It was far away, three months traveling to my place back.
I said, if they gonna-- if they going to kill, get me now-- and I had a lot of money-- they going to make hamburgers of me, or steaks.
I start running back to the train.
I start running back.
I didn't have time to go down and grab anything.
So when I started running back, I couldn't catch the train.
A guy jumped on.
His hand was bandaged.
He was wearing a Russian uniform.
And he had revolver in here.
And he held onto the train with his hand like that.
He handed me the hand.
And he grabbed me.
He pushed me up.
He brought me up on the train.
When we came in the train, and I stood on my place, and he came over.
He said, who are you?
What are you?
And I told him who I am, that I am going here and here, and I have a sister living there.
And I came to sell things.
And so now I'm going back.
And somehow, he started talking to me, that maybe I'll go with them because there was something then that England and Russia made an agreement, that they're going to let their people out.
And these people can go to England, that was in Russia.
So they took only the Goyim, not the Hedin.
And this guy got himself a little paper.
You know, you could make for money, everything in there, and the other one.
And they both went on that boat.
Oh, not yet.
Because I said goodbye to them.
And I went down in Tashkent.
And they, I don't know where they were.
But he told me this story later.
His name was Mendel.
His real name is Lippa-- Leo, Lippa.
But in there, for some kind of reason-- and I'll tell you later the reason-- that he-- he was caught in something and he had to change his name.
So for a few ruble, you change your name.
So I came back.
And I came to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Tashkent.
The city was the first where I went then, is Tashkent.
And then I had to go to Samarkand to see my girlfriend.
So when I came to Tashkent, I bought tobacco.
In Chkalov, because it was very good.
Because the Uzbeks were taking all kind of opiums and all kind, and the tobacco they like, too.
And as I went, the first thing on the market, I want to eat something.
There's little guys-- they call them [NON-ENGLISH]-- they grabbed that bag with the tobacco from me right away, away.
And they run away.
And I wanted to run after them.
But I didn't know which way they went.
So I still want to buy.
And there was big tables set on the market.
And people were selling some sour milk, a piece of bread, whatever.
And I went close there.
And my money, I had in here.
Because I knew that here, nobody can.
And I took out some money.
And I stand at the table.
And just pushed my way to the table.
I didn't know they're sitting on the bottom there.
And I'm buying whatever I want to buy.
And when I paid for that piece of bread with some milk, I don't know what it was then.
And I moved away.
They caught this apron, what I was going to go to university in it.
And they cut it out exactly like this, all my pockets.
They thought I had money there or something.
And when I came out, I didn't see them.
But I was without clothes.
So I had what I bought in Russia.
Somehow I manage.
And I came back.
I came back to Samarkand, and I met my sister.
It's not a sister, what I called her, because we were together all the time.
She was happy to see me.
I brought money.
Now we can survive and we'll be all right.
I was very sorry that I didn't went with these two guys.
Because I could have been in England.
And there I came back to the same where I am.
But I couldn't leave her on account of the money.
And I think I was in love already, with him.
And he came, he wanted to take me with.
So, I-- I was very-- I didn't know what was doing with me.
I was going around and I was dreaming.
And I said, the war never finished.
And we are going to die in here.
You said your girlfriend got very impatient with you.
Yes, yes, yeah.
Anyways, she used to yell at me, scream.
But we still are going out on the market.
We were doing that business.
And one day, I went out on the market.
And I see there is a group of Russian people-- men, women.
They gather around, all these people, where they handling the selling the things.
They call us [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] you know, it means make money.
It was against the government.
And they say, look, you all Jewish people here.
You don't go to the war.
Our husbands are killed.
And you're just living.
We're going to kill you all.
And I was standing from far away.
And I start moving over.
And I saw a guy, comes in there.
And dressed up in the military.
Because that saved them from a lot of problems.
And he takes out his gun.
And he says to them, look, I am Jewish.
And I was in the army, in the Russian army.
And if you're not going to leave those people here, I'm going to shoot you all like the dogs.
And they all went away.
